scenarios.title: Tanker leak at I-95 mile 22
scenarios.context: You arrive on a single engine. Light mist drifting across the southbound lanes. A bystander says she smelled fuel. You see UN 1203 placards. Wind is from the SW at ~10 mph. Two vehicles are stopped within 30m of the tanker.
scenarios.outcomeType: Decision sequence — isolate, communicate, request resources

steps.prompt: First 60 seconds. What do you do?

options.label: Approach the tanker to read the BOL
options.feedback: You closed the gap before establishing isolation — this is how responders become patients.

options.label: Establish 50m isolation upwind, hold position
options.feedback: Good. You bought yourself a thinking margin and protected the bystanders downwind.

options.label: Begin moving stopped vehicles
options.feedback: Reasonable instinct, but isolation comes first. Moving people while exposed is a coin flip.

steps.prompt: Comms check. What's your sitrep to dispatch?
steps.modelAnswer: Engine 4 on scene I-95 SB mile 22, single tanker UN 1203, active leak with vapor, wind SW 10, 50m isolation set, requesting hazmat 1 and PD for traffic.

steps.prompt: Hazmat 1 is 9 minutes out. A driver in the isolation zone steps out and walks toward you.

options.label: Wave him toward you, away from tanker
options.feedback: Right call. Move him crosswind, not toward the source, and document exposure time.

options.label: Tell him over PA to stay in his vehicle
options.feedback: Defensible — but a stationary vehicle in a vapor plume isn't a safe shelter.

options.label: Send your partner in to retrieve him
options.feedback: You just doubled exposure without PPE. Hazmat 1 will arrive to two patients.

steps.prompt: You're handing off to the arriving IC. What do you lead with?

options.label: Location, product, isolation, resources, life safety
options.feedback: Clean handoff. The new IC starts thinking, not catching up.

options.label: What you've already done in chronological order
options.feedback: Useful, but slower. Lead with the picture, then the actions.

options.label: Your uncertainty about the wind direction
options.feedback: Lead with what you know. Save uncertainties for the second beat.

scenarios.title: 78F, altered, fever — triage decision
scenarios.context: 78-year-old female brought in by family for confusion since this morning. Vitals: T 38.6, HR 112, RR 24, BP 98/56, SpO2 95% RA. Family says she's been on antibiotics for a UTI for 2 days.

steps.prompt: Initial triage. What do you flag?

options.label: qSOFA positive — escalate now
options.feedback: RR 24, altered, SBP <100 — three of three. Don't wait on cultures to escalate.

options.label: Possible UTI worsening, route to fast-track
options.feedback: She meets qSOFA criteria. Fast-track delays the hour-1 bundle.

options.label: Hold and reassess in 15 minutes
options.feedback: Watchful waiting here costs you the bundle window.

steps.modelAnswer: Lactate, blood cultures x2, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, and prep norepinephrine if MAP stays <65 after fluids.

steps.prompt: After 1L crystalloid: BP 92/54, MAP 66. Lactate 3.4. What now?

options.label: Continue fluids to 30 mL/kg total, reassess MAP
options.feedback: Right path. You haven't completed the bundle's fluid target yet.

options.label: Start norepinephrine immediately
options.feedback: Defensible if she's not tolerating fluids — but MAP 66 buys you time to finish the resuscitation.

options.label: Hold fluids, recheck in 30 minutes
options.feedback: You'd be undertreating an actively septic patient.
